proof_stages.label: Planar-to-nonplanar motivation and program
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames nonplanar on-shell diagrams, or nonplanar plabic graphs, as objects that lose planar positivity and ordering but still retain amplitude information, motivating a search for new combinatorial, geometric, and symmetry structures.

proof_stages.label: Planar positive-Grassmannian and plabic-graph toolkit
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews G(k,n), positivity, cells and boundaries, positive boundary-measurement parametrizations by plabic graphs, and the reduced-graph identity moves that preserve the associated positroid cell.

proof_stages.label: Planar on-shell diagrams as Grassmannian forms
proof_stages.summary: The amplitude setup is translated into spinor-helicity variables, dlog forms, and delta-function constraints, yielding reduced 2n-4 dimensional plabic cells as leading singularities for tree and loop amplitude data.

proof_stages.label: Nonplanar extension of boundary measurement and canonical forms
proof_stages.summary: After explaining why momentum twistors, positivity, a single ordering, and the known amplituhedron fail nonplanarly, the talk retains boundary measurement, edge-variable dlog forms, and planar positroid boundary pieces as the working framework.

proof_stages.label: G(2,n) triplet forms, moves, and top-cell classification
proof_stages.summary: For G(2,n), Parke-Taylor factors form a basis of dlog forms, nonplanar diagrams admit a triplet determinant computation, square and sphere moves preserve the form, and reduced top cells are conjecturally those without vanishing Plücker coordinates, leaving an open invariant-label problem.

proof_stages.label: Numerators as poles at infinity
proof_stages.summary: The speaker argues from internal on-shell momenta that numerator zeros of nonplanar canonical forms coincide with conditions sending loop momenta to infinity, a relation checked in many G(2,n) and some G(3,n) examples.

proof_stages.label: G(3,n) leading singularities and nonadjacent BCFW geometry
proof_stages.summary: Moving to G(3,n) removes tiling by ordered positive parts and makes the C-matrix solution nontrivial; planar BCFW cells are special three-line configurations, while nonadjacent BCFW bridges give controlled nonplanar configurations with analogous numerator and pole-at-infinity behavior.

proof_stages.label: Systematic G(3,6) data and open nonplanar geometry
proof_stages.summary: Existing enumerations of nonplanar G(3,6) diagrams reveal only selected nonconvex geometries, intersection-point configurations, square-root cases, and non-ball topology, pointing to open questions about classification, hidden symmetries, nonplanar integrands, and a possible nonplanar amplituhedron.
